coredata.dc:title: Unveiling Nano-Resveratrol induced by oil-in-water binding to human haemoglobin in the presence of holo-transferrin as binary and ternary systems: Spectroscopy, molecular dynamic and hemoglobin oxygenation approaches
coredata.dc:creator: Masoudnia, Shiva
Motavalizadehkakhki, Alireza
Mehrzad, Jamshid
Hoseini, Malihesadat
Chamani, Jamshidkhan
coredata.dc:description: Resveratrol (RES) is a member of polyphenols compounds capable of performing antioxidant activities and exhibiting anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties. This study attempted to increase the bioavailability and solubility of RES through an O/W nano-emulsion process. The performance of synthesized Nano-RES with hemoglobin (Hb) and human holo transferrin (HTF) as the binary and ternary systems was investigated by the means of various spectroscopic techniques and cancer cell experiments. Fluorescence quenching measurement configured the static quenching mechanism of Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) interaction with Nano-RES complexes. According to the results, the measurements for binding constant of Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES complexes were observed to be (6.62 ± 0.08) × 104 and (6.37 ± 0.09) × 104 M−1 at 298 K respectively. Thermodynamic parameters revealed the participation of electrostatic forces throughout the binding of ligands and proteins. Furthermore, the resonance light scattering signal of Hb and Hb-HTF in the presence of Nano-RES displayed the formation and production of complexes in larger sizes. According to the data of far-UV CD and UV/vis, the secondary structure of proteins was altered by the interaction of Nano-RES with both proteins. The results of melting point assessment confirmed the impact of Nano-RES on increasing the thermal stability of Hb-HTF complex as a ternary system. As the different behavior of Hb and Hb-HTF in the presence of Nano-RES concentrations was approved by the conductivity analysis, the distances between donor and acceptor was determined by fluorescence resonance energy transfer measurements (FRET) to be 1.68 nm and 1.77 nm for Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES complexes, respectively. Molecular dynamic study was carried out to investigate the binding interactions of RES to Hb and Hb-HTF complex and was followed by MD simulation under the calculation of 100 ns. Lastly, we evaluated the cytotoxicity of Nano-RES in different concentrations on A549 and BEAS-2B cells, which increased the rate of apoptosis in A549 cells.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM)
Fluorescence quenching of Hb and Hb-HTF in the presence of Nano-RES

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support of the Research Council of the Mashhad Branch, and Neyshabur Branch Islamic Azad University , are gratefully acknowledged. The authors thank Dr.Ljungburg for the English editing.
The financial support of the Research Council of the Mashhad Branch, and Neyshabur Branch, Islamic Azad University are appreciated and acknowledged.

The present study attempted to inquire into the binding of two well-known carrier proteins, Hb and HTF, in the appearance of Nano-RES to identify their structural and functional alterations. For this purpose, we exerted the data of different spectroscopic techniques including fluorescence spectroscopy, circular dichroism, conductometry, isothermal titration calorimetry, and MD simulation measurements next to conducting cell cancer experiments and oxygenation assay. Moreover, we determined the suitable approaches for varying drug binding modes such as the sites, affinity, and mechanism of bindings. In the following, the dynamic behavior and stability of our complexes [Hb-RES, (Hb-HTF) RES] were evaluated through the combined application of molecular docking, which is one of the most important computational approaches, with MD simulation in an explicit solvent environment [19]. This survey indicated the useful bioavailability and biological effects of the drug release for pharmaceutical fields. The results of cell cancer experiments on A549 and BEAS-2B as the alveolar and bronchial epithelial cell lines were conducted by the potential of Nano-RES in enhancing the rate of cell viability and apoptosis.The financial support of the Research Council of the Mashhad Branch, and Neyshabur Branch Islamic Azad University, are gratefully acknowledged. The authors thank Dr.Ljungburg for the English editing. Ethical Approval. There is none to be disclosed. The financial support of the Research Council of the Mashhad Branch, and Neyshabur Branch, Islamic Azad University are appreciated and acknowledged. Availability of data and materials. Data available on request from the authors. The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author, [author initials], upon reasonable request.

head.ce:title: Unveiling Nano-Resveratrol induced by oil-in-water binding to human haemoglobin in the presence of holo-transferrin as binary and ternary systems: Spectroscopy, molecular dynamic and hemoglobin oxygenation approaches

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Resveratrol (RES) is a member of polyphenols compounds capable of performing antioxidant activities and exhibiting anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties. This study attempted to increase the bioavailability and solubility of RES through an O/W nano-emulsion process. The performance of synthesized Nano-RES with hemoglobin (Hb) and human holo transferrin (HTF) as the binary and ternary systems was investigated by the means of various spectroscopic techniques and cancer cell experiments. Fluorescence quenching measurement configured the static quenching mechanism of Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) interaction with Nano-RES complexes. According to the results, the measurements for binding constant of Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES complexes were observed to be (6.62 ± 0.08) × 104 and (6.37 ± 0.09) × 104 M−1 at 298 K respectively. Thermodynamic parameters revealed the participation of electrostatic forces throughout the binding of ligands and proteins. Furthermore, the resonance light scattering signal of Hb and Hb-HTF in the presence of Nano-RES displayed the formation and production of complexes in larger sizes. According to the data of far-UV CD and UV/vis, the secondary structure of proteins was altered by the interaction of Nano-RES with both proteins. The results of melting point assessment confirmed the impact of Nano-RES on increasing the thermal stability of Hb-HTF complex as a ternary system. As the different behavior of Hb and Hb-HTF in the presence of Nano-RES concentrations was approved by the conductivity analysis, the distances between donor and acceptor was determined by fluorescence resonance energy transfer measurements (FRET) to be 1.68 nm and 1.77 nm for Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES complexes, respectively. Molecular dynamic study was carried out to investigate the binding interactions of RES to Hb and Hb-HTF complex and was followed by MD simulation under the calculation of 100 ns. Lastly, we evaluated the cytotoxicity of Nano-RES in different concentrations on A549 and BEAS-2B cells, which increased the rate of apoptosis in A549 cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular recognition is one of the main goals in experimental, theoretical, and medicinal fields of chemistry, in which intermolecular interactions are the focus of attention in physiological and pathological processes [1]. Hence, the comprehension of biological activities is directly related to identifying the existing phenomena at molecular level, such as the binding of drugs to enzymes or protein receptors, in order to provide useful data for the design and presentation of new drug targets in pharmaceutical fields [2]. The selection of proteins as targets for drug binding studies is attributed to their stance as a fundamental group of biological macromolecules due to their various functions in living bodies such as molecules transportation, body tissues regulation, and catalysis of biochemical reactions [3,4].

Hemoglobin (Hb) is known as an iron-containing protein in blood that is mainly involved in the transportation of O2 from the lung to tissues in mammals [5]. They are created in bone marrow cells and converted into red blood cells. Next to the molecular weight of 64500 Da, each protein is consisted of four subunits, two α-subunits (α1 and α2), and two β-subunits (β1 and β2) in the form of 7 and 8 helices, called A-H, respectively. Considering their similarities in structure and size, each subunit contains a binding pocket formed by the E and F helices. Heme group contains a protoporphyrin ring that is centered on a ferrous (Fe2+) atom and surrounded by four nitrogen atoms (Fig. 1 ) [6–8]. In terms of Hb characteristics, this carrier protein is exerted to determine the oxygenation changes of the interaction between the protein and applied ligand.

Another abundant protein in blood plasma is known to be human serum transferrin (HTF) with a molecular weight of ∼ 80 kDa [9]. This protein is composed of two homologous lobes (N-terminal lobe and a C-terminal lobe) while each lobe is subdivided into two liable domains for metal ions and minerals transportation [10]. Additionally, HTF is comprised of 8 Trp residues and 19 intra-chain disulfide bonds with a metal-binding site, which facilitate its binding to two ferric ions per molecule for performing deliveries throughout the entire body. Hence, the direct interaction of drugs with HTF has been extensively studied due to its abilities in iron transportation by receptor-mediated endocytosis that led to its stance as are markable candidate for drug delivery in biomedical applications [11,12].

Resveratrol (RES) (trans-3,4,5-trihydroxystilbene) is a natural polyphenol member of stilbene family with a significant role in various biological activities such as functioning against glycation, oxidative stress, inflammation, neurodegeneration, and different types of cancer [13,14]. This compound contains two phenol rings (monophenol and diphenol) that are connected by a double styrene bond and three hydroxyl groups, which participate in free radicals elimination, metal chelation, and macromolecules interactions. RES is found in the biologically more active forms of cis and trans isomeric in nature (Fig. 2 ). Upon its exposure to light, the trans iso-form of RES is converted into cis iso-form through a photo isomerization process. The high tolerance of this substance is believed to be a hopeful approach for preventing the occurrence of many diseases such as diabetes. In addition, RES has revealed many other characteristics including antioxidative, anti-inflammatory, anticancer, antimicrobial, anti-neurodegenerative, and estrogenic properties, as well as the ability to regulate innate and adaptive immunity by interacting with several molecular targets [15,16]. The data of pharmacokinetic analysis pointed out the low bioavailability and solubility of this material in aqueous environments. Therefore, its conversion into nano-emulsion form is suitable for increasing its solubility and cellular uptakes [17–19].

In recent years, the extended development of nano-emulsion methods has reached a wide range of clinical applications. Emulsion is a type of colloid in liquids that implicate the dispersion of small droplets of a liquid (such as oil) in other liquids (such as water) without dissolving in each other. An emulsion is consisted of two immiscible liquids (usually oil and water), one of which is dispersed in the form of small droplets within the other. The dispersed liquid is called the dispersed or internal phase while the liquid around the droplets is labeled as the continuous, closed, or external phase [13–15]. These materials are categorized into coarse emulsions, micro-emulsions, and nano-emulsions in accordance with the size and stability of droplets [16]. As a common fact, the dispersion of oil droplets in an aqueous phase form is known as an oil-in-water (o/w) emulsions system that can be used as a hydrophobic active delivery agent. Nano-emulsions are identified as a kinetic stability with no gravitational separation and aggregation of droplets due to the reduced force among the small droplets [17,18].

The present study attempted to inquire into the binding of two well-known carrier proteins, Hb and HTF, in the appearance of Nano-RES to identify their structural and functional alterations. For this purpose, we exerted the data of different spectroscopic techniques including fluorescence spectroscopy, circular dichroism, conductometry, isothermal titration calorimetry, and MD simulation measurements next to conducting cell cancer experiments and oxygenation assay. Moreover, we determined the suitable approaches for varying drug binding modes such as the sites, affinity, and mechanism of bindings. In the following, the dynamic behavior and stability of our complexes [Hb-RES, (Hb-HTF) RES] were evaluated through the combined application of molecular docking, which is one of the most important computational approaches, with MD simulation in an explicit solvent environment [19]. This survey indicated the useful bioavailability and biological effects of the drug release for pharmaceutical fields. The results of cell cancer experiments on A549 and BEAS-2B as the alveolar and bronchial epithelial cell lines were conducted by the potential of Nano-RES in enhancing the rate of cell viability and apoptosis.

Hb, HTF, RES, and all the other substances were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO. USA). Based on the molecular weights of proteins (Hb and HTF), the stock solution (3 × 10-3 mM) was prepared in a 50 mM potassium phosphate buffer solution at pH = 7.4, while Nano-RES was provided through the nano-emulsion Oil-In-Water procedure [20]. The prepared stock solution was kept in a refrigerator at the temperature of 4 °C in dark.

An oil-in-water (o/w) nano-emulsion method was elaborated to enhance the solubility, bioavailability, and stability of RES. In order to arrange Nano-RES via the oil-in-water (o/w) emulsion method, the oily phase was completed by shaking olive oil, tween 80 as the surfactant, and span 60 as the co-surfactant for 30 min at the ratios of 1:2:2 w/w/w. Thereafter, 320 mg of RES was added to the oily phase and stirred continuously at 40 °C by 3000 rpm for 24 h in order to force the dissolution into its equilibrium state. Each vial was centrifuged through the usage of Hettich, Germany, at 4000 rpm subsequent to waiting for 24 h and equilibrating at 25 °C. Then, the un-dissolved solution was omitted by exerting a membrane filter of 0.45 µm, which was sited in a sonicator bath (Sonorex Digitec, Model: Andelin 35 kHz, Germany) for 1 h to acquire a clear yellow homogeneous solution. Once 100 µL of the solution was spread in 50 mL of double distilled water, the droplet size of Nano-RES was determined after dilution. In order to optimize the size of prepared Nano-RES, the oil and surfactant mixture were blended at different volume ratios of 1:1:1, 2:1:1, 1:2:1 and 2:1:2 [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM)
ce:section.ce:para: A scanning electron microscope (SEM) examines the focused stream of electrons over the sample surface and collects the various signals generated using specialized detectors. This technique provides the microstructure and morphology of the specimens in two-dimensional and three-dimensional images. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) is a developed technology that performs a higher resolution and magnification along with high-quality images. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is relied on light in the visible spectrum, which can represent the detail at the atomic level by magnifying nano-scale structures up to 50 million times [21,22].

The dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique is excreted to determine the size distribution profile of small particles in suspension that can be detectable through the mechanism of light scattering from a laser through the colloidal solution. Hence, to determine the nano particle size of resveratrol, a Zeta sizer-Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, UK) was applied [23].
UV/vis absorption spectra were registered on a V-630 (Jasco, Japan) spectrophotometer with a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette at 298 K.

Fluorescence evaluations were accomplished by the means of a Hitachi F-2500 spectrophotometer (Japan) that was supplied with 1.0 cm quartz, a 150 W xenon lampas the light source, and a thermostat bath. The excitation wavelengths were adjusted at 280 nm and the recorded emission wavelengths were settled between the range of 300 and 600 nm. The voltage of photomultiplier tube (PMT) was arranged at 700 V with the scanning speed of 1200 nm/min [24]. Furthermore, the quenching data were assessed subsequent to correcting the fluorescence intensity in order to induce internal filtering and dilution effects. The fluorescence measurements of Hb and Hb-HTF complex were done at the appearance of Nano-RES in the forms of binary and ternary systems. All of the experiments were carried out at the three different temperatures of 298, 303, and 308 K.

The spectroscopic microenvironmental behavior of Trp and Tyr residues were revealed by excreting the simultaneous scanning of excitation and emission monochromators as the wavelengths were fixed at Δλ = 60 nm and 15 nm, respectively. Hence, synchronous fluorescence measurements were performed for Hb-Nano-RES as the binary and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES complex as the ternary systems.

Resonance light scattering (RLS) is an acute and selective method for gathering data about the size and shape of supra-molecular assemblies of chromophores templates [25]. The measurement of RLS spectrum required the adjustment of both excitation and emission spectra in a range of 280 to 600 nm at Δλ = 0 nm. The RLS titration experiments were conducted in similar conditions to that of fluorescence measurements.

The secondary structural changes of Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES complexes were acquired by Jasco-815 spectropolarimeter 815 (Japan spectroscopic Company, Tokyo, Japan) equipped with a Jasco 2-syringe titrator. In addition, the CD spectra data were obtained through the application of identical protein and different ligand concentrations in a 1.0 mm path length quartz cuvette by the interval wavelength range of 190 to 250 nm and the scanning speed of 100 nm/min at 20 ± 0.1 °C. The instruments were calibrated with ammonium d-10-camphorsulfonic acid. In order to analyze the content of far UV circular dichroism spectra, the measured CD spectra data were configured through the utilization of CDNN and SELCON II deconvolution programs, which contained five different secondary structures including α-helix, β-sheet parallel, anti-parallel, turn, and random coil. For exploring changes in the secondary structure, the Far-UV CD spectra were obtained over a wavelength range of 240–190 nm in the absence and presence of different concentrations of Nano-RES at the same condition as above. In all titration experiments, the dilution factor of ligand titration was corrected. The reported values are expressed by the mean residue molar ellipticity [θ] asthe following [26]:

The effect of temperature on Hb and Hb-HTF complex in the appearance of Nano-RES was examined through a spectrophotometer that was equipped with a thermocouple. The melting plot were acquired by the proteins absorbance at 280 nm against temperature, while the transition midpoints represented the Tm (melting temperature) values for both Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES complexes as the binary and ternary systems.

Conductometry technique is used to analyze ionic species and chemical reactions [29]. In this regard, the electric conductivity of Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES complex was specified through the performed conductometry measurement by Metrohm, Germany. Next to calibrating the cells by the usage of potassium chloride 1 M, all the stock solutions were prepared by reagent-grade (Merck) and potassium thiocyanate, 30 % hydrogen peroxide, and sodium hydroxide copper sulfate pentahydrate. This experiment required the application of re-distilled water as well.

Oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve represents the association of bounded oxygen to the amount of Hb and the partial pressure (PO2) of oxygen in blood, which is exhibited through a sigmoidal curve [30]. The measurement is completed by a blood gas device with a silver anode/platinum cathode system in an electrolyte solution. Also, the mixture was detached from the solution by a semi-permeable membrane.

The technique of in vitro release behaviour can provide data on the optimization of in vivo performance, principally drug release or dose depletion [31]. It implicated the usage of dialysis membrane to assess the release of Nano-RES, prepared by o/w binding, for the cases of Hb and Hb-HTF complexes, RES-loaded mesoporous silica nanoparticles (RES-MSNs), and RES-loaded solid lipid nanoparticles (RES-SLNs). To prepare the solution, 3.0 mL of each composition was placed into a dialysis sac (12 kDa) and mixed with 3.0 mL of simulated gastric fluid (SGF) composed of HCl, NaCl, and pepsin in the concentration of 3.2 mg/mL at a pH of 1.2. The bag was immersed in 150 mL of release medium, consisted of enzyme-free SGF and ethanol, to be incubated on a shaker at 37 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, the mixture was filled with 6 mL of simulated intestinal fluid (SIF) composed of NaOH, monobasic potassium phosphate, and pancreatin in a concentration of 10 mg/mL at the pH of 7.5. The process was continued by situating the dialysis bag in 150 mL of fresh release medium with enzyme-free SIF and ethanol to create the proper conditions for controlling the poor solubility of Nano-RES in water. A small amount of external release medium was prepared in 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 h to define the amount of released Nano-RES, while the measurement was completed at the wavelength of 235 nm. The released Nano-RES concentration behavior was accounted through the Nano-RES standard curves that were performed through a similar release medium [32].

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a practical and functional technique for quantifying the distances of two conjugated molecules to different fluorophores [33]. Hence, a Jasco 815 double-beam spectrophotometer was exerted to acquire the absorption spectra of Nano-RES in the ranges of 200–700 nm at room temperature. The excitation wavelength was adjusted at 295 nm and the energy transfer was accounted by overlapping the UV absorption spectrum of Nano-RES with the fluorescence emission spectrum of Hb and Hb-HTF complex.

Isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) is used to detect the binding of two molecules that release or absorb heat upon binding [34]. The binding energy of Nano-RES with Hb and Hb-HTF complex was identified through the means of an isothermal titration calorimeter (TA Instruments Limited, New Castle, DE, USA). Then, the specified amounts of proteins and ligand solution were injected into the sample cell to obtain the ITC data. The collected results were adjusted into a single set of identical site model through ITC Run software. This method can be also applied to determine certain thermodynamic parameters such as affinity (Ka), enthalpy (ΔH0), entropy (ΔS0), and stoichiometry (n).
The experimental compound of this study was evaluated by Swiss ADME web server (https://www.swissadme.ch/index.php) in order to examine the pharmacokinetic parameters.

Toxicological endpoints were predicted by ProTox-II online server as a toxicity lab that can be accessed at https://tox-new.charite.de/protox_II/. The application of computational toxicity predictions through machine learning-based techniques can reduce the need for animal testings.

ProTox-II implicates the usage of machine learning-based algorithms to predict a variety of toxicological endpoints including acute toxicity that provides the estimated LD50 of a compound, cytotoxicity, carcinogenicity, hepatotoxicity, mutagenicity, immunotoxicity, and adverse outcome (Tox21) pathways. Table 2 displays the prediction outcomes of these endpoints obtained by the compound canonical smile [35].
The performed computational docking studies by MOE™ tool (2022) helped in comprehending the binding mode of created complexes between Hb, Hb-HTF, and RES.

The obtained RES from PubChem website was prepared by MOE 2022 and then saved as a PDB file for the docking process. Also, the Protein Data Bank website was used to download the crystal structure of human hemoglobin and holo-transferrin (PDB code 2D60 and 1SUV respectively). All of the water molecules of proteins were deleted next to the adjustment of hydrogens atoms and charges.

Docking studies were completed by MOE with the application of 300 poses for Hb-RES. This process required the default settings of sustaining the ligand conformers with the highest and best scores among the 10 and London dG as the scoring function. According to the energetic considerations (GBVI/WSA), the best poses with the lowest binding energies were chosen for the scoring configuration of ligand-target complexes [36].

GROMACS 2018.2 package with a united-atom force field (CHARMM36) was exerted for all the MD simulations, which also required the received topology file of the small molecule from SWISS Param [39]. The system was neutralized with Na and Cl ions in prior to conducting MD for energy minimization and position restraint. Subsequent to preparing a water box for all the systems, counter-ions were used to neutralize the systems and the steepest descent method was employed to minimize the energy for 50,000 steps. The application of NVT was followed by NPT ensemble to complete every simulation under periodic boundary conditions [40]. In the end, 100 ns of MD runs were produced to facilitate the movement of all the molecules in every direction in accordance with a conventional Newtonian leap-frog MD integrator [41].

Cytotoxicity evaluation was done on the two A549 and BEAS-2B cell lines, which were kept in a RPMI 1640 medium (containing:10 % fetal bovine serum, 100 U/mL of penicillin, and 100 mg/L of streptomycin). The applied temperature for maintaining the cells was 37 °C with the moisture conditioning of 5 % CO2 and 95 % air. Hence, the cytotoxicity of Nano-RES on these two cell lines was assessed by MTT assay [42]. For this purpose, the cells were situated in 96-well plates and treated with different concentrations of Nano-RES (0–0.01 mg/mL) for 48 h. Thereafter, the cells were incubated at 37 °C in a humidified chamber, and 5 mg/mL of MTT reagent in PBS (Phosphate buffered saline) was added to each well to perform incubation for 4 h. Once the medium was removed, 150 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was dissolved with the formazan that was created by metabolically viable cells. Ultimately, the absorbance was identified through an infinite M200 Pro microplate reader at the specified wavelength of 570 nm.

Fig. 3 A represents the FE-SEM image of the morphological changes of Nano-RES with high resolution which in spherical shape. Moreover, Fig. 3B shows the TEM micrographs of Nano-RES in spherical particles which was affirmed by the obtained data from the FE-SEM image.
Fig. 4 demonstrates the estimated Z-Average of the arranged Nano-RES by the means of dynamic light scattering (DLS) that can be acquired about 60 nm.

To confirm the structural alterations of Hb with the addition of Nano-RES in the absence and presence of HTF, the UV/vis absorption spectra were registered. Fig. 5 shows the UV/vis absorption spectra of Hb and Hb-HTF from 202 to 502 nm in phosphate buffer solution in the presence of different Nano-RES concentrations. As shown in Fig. 5, the absorbance at 208 nm of Hb and Hb-HTF decreased with the addition of Nano-RES and a slight red shift of interaction between Nano-RES with Hb and Hb-HTF can be observed from the UV/vis absorption spectra. The two evidence clearly indicated the interaction between Nano-RES with Hb and Hb-HTF, the red-shift also signified that the polypeptide chain of Hb and Hb-HTF molecules extended more, and the polarity was strengthened, inducing the change of α-helical structure of the proteins [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of Hb and Hb-HTF in the presence of Nano-RES
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence spectroscopy is an influential method that can be used in analytical science fields due to its high sensitivity and selective properties in pharmaceutical industry to provide valuable structural and functional information on macromolecules. Considering the reliance of this technique on the existing intrinsic chromophores in many native protein sequences, fluorescence spectroscopy measurements can illuminate samples with short wavelengths in the visible/ultraviolet wavelength regions. Also, there is the possibility of the electrons of a sample to absorb a part of the incident light and consequently reach higher energy levels. [44,45]. We investigated the interaction between Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES as the binary and ternary systems through fluorescence spectroscopy, and observed the binding affinity change of Nano-RES to Hb in the presence of HTF. Fig. 6 A and B demonstrate the fluorescence emission spectra of Hb and Hb-HTF complex in the presence of Nano-RES, respectively, at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm. Accordingly, the fluorescence intensity of both systems was gradually decreased as the concentration of Nano-RES was increased (0–0.01 mM). This observation implies the occurrence of a quenching phenomenon by the interactions between the ligand and both proteins. In conformity to the spectrum in Fig. 6A, Hb induced a slight red shift in the presence of Nano-RES and indicated the more polar state of fluorophore microenvironment, whereas a slight blue shift in the case of Hb-HTF was observed that suggested the altered interaction behavior of Nano-RES with Hb in the appearance of HTF (Fig. 6B). In regard to quenching processes, the intrinsic fluorescence of a protein is produced from the three aromatic amino acids of Trp, Tyr, and Phe that are regularly categorized as dynamic or static quenching mechanisms. Evidently, dynamic quenching relies on the interaction between the donor and acceptor molecules in which the donor fluorophore transfers energy to the acceptor and emits light or entirely absorbs the energy. This process implicates electron excitation in prior to the quenching process, while static quenching mechanism involves the generation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex with a specific absorption spectrum through the formation of an intramolecular interaction between the receptor and quencher. The quenching processes were analyzed through the following Stern-Volmer equation [46]:
(4)
F 0 / F = 1 + k q
τ 0
[ Q ]
=
1 + K s v
Q
In which, F0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensity in the absence and appearance of quencher, Ksv expresses the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, kq stands for the rate of quenching constant, τ0 (10-8 s) is the normal fluorescence lifetime without a quencher, and [Q] represents the quencher concentration. The Stern-Volmer plot of
F 0
/ F
against the quencher concentration at three different temperatures (298, 303, and 308 K) in the cases of Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES systems are presented in Fig. 6C and D. The slopes of the Stern-Volmer plots at three different temperatures can determine the Stern-Volmer constants, which indicate the tendency of Nano-RES to bind to Hb in the absence and presence of HTF as binary and ternary systems. The binding constants of Ksv and Kb are both equilibrium constants, this indicates that the binding between Nano-RES and hemoglobin in the absence and presence of HTF is of the type of physical forces such as electrostatic forces, van der Waals and hydrophobic forces. This clearly shows that the binding of Nano-RES to Hb is reversible in the absence and presence of HTF. The same issue shows the role of Hb and HTF proteins as carriers for Nano-RES.

The linear curves demonstrated the occurrence of a single type of either static or dynamic quenching. Table 1
indicates a reduction in the Ksv values of both binary and ternary systems as a result of increasing the applied temperature, suggesting the dominance of static quenching mechanism throughout the interaction of Hb and Hb-HTF complex in the presence of Nano-RES. The comparison of Ksv values at three various temperatures of binary and ternary system pointed out a reduction and indicated the effect of HTF presence on decreasing the interaction affinity of Nano-RES to Hb. The use of antioxidant compounds such as Nano-RES as food supplements is valuable when it does not cause too much change in the carrier proteins in the blood and does not change the function of the carrier proteins. Therefore, the binding constants that indicate the binding tendency are important from the point of view that the ligand does not change the structure of the carrier proteins.

The comprehension of molecular identification processes of small ligands and biological macromolecules requires data on the entire properties of energetics and thermodynamic bindings including free energy of binding (ΔG0), enthalpy (ΔH0)and entropy (ΔS0) of binding, and heat capacity change (ΔCp). This information can determine the involved forces throughout the interaction, which include electrostatic, hydrophobic interactions, van der Waals forces, and hydrogen bonds. Hence, the gathered data were analyzed through the van Hoff equation [47]:
(5)
ln
K b
= -
Δ
H
0
RT
+
Δ
S
0
R
In which
K b
expresses the binding constant at three specific temperatures (298 K, 303 K, and 308 K), R stands for the gas constant, and T represents the temperature. Therefore, the values of Gibbs free change (ΔG0) were configured as the following [48]:
(6)
Δ G 0 =
Δ H 0
- T Δ S 0
The values of Kb, ΔH0, ΔS0, and ΔG0 are listed in Table 1. The varying behavior of both constants was represented by the differences of Ksv and Kb values as that of Ksv were related to the dynamic process and the Kb was correlated with static mechanism. First, the high values of the binding constants show that these binding can be done in the in vivo at binary and ternary systems. As can be seen from Table 1, the Ksv values of the interaction between Nano-RES and Hb in the presence of HTF are lower than binary system that reveal that the binding affinity of Nano-RES to Hb decrease in the presence of HTF. Therefore, in the presence of HTF, the conformational changes in Hb occurred that the binding sites of Nano-RES are hidden in Hb. In Table 1, both Ksv and Kb values decrease with enhancement of temperatures that revealed the combine of static and dynamic quenching occurred. The negative changes of ΔG0 energy signified the spontaneous mode of binding process. Fig. 7 exhibits theVan’t Hoff plot of Hb and Hb-HTF interaction in the appearance of Nano-RES. Hence, the diagram of lnKb versus 1/T was drawn for both of the binary and ternary systems at the three varying temperatures of 298, 303, and 308 K. The ΔH0 and ΔS0 values were calculated through the slope and intercept of linear van't Hoff plot, respectively. The acquired results of ΔH0 < 0 and ΔS0 > 0 from the cases of Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES complexes as the binary and ternary systems suggested the involvement of electrostatic molecular forces throughout the interaction. In comparison to the binary system, Table 1 displays a slight decrease in the ΔH0 and ΔS0 values of ternary system, which points out the reduced contribution of electrostatic forces in the interaction of Hb-HTF complex in the appearance of Nano-RES. According to this hypothesis, the induced reduction in the binding affinity of ternary system is caused by the pervasive effects of electrostatic repulsion.

Synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy (SFS) is an oncoming tool for enabling an efficient increase in analytical selectivity and high fluorimetric sensitivity through the simultaneous scanning of excitation and emission monochromators [49]. Hence, it is known as an interesting technique for the simultaneous determination of complex multi-compound samples. The advantages of this measurement include spectral simplification, thin bandwidth, decreased scattering interference resolution, and enhanced scattering intervention [50]. In coordination to Fig. 8 A and B, the fluorescence intensity of Hb and Hb-HTF complex were decreased upon the binding of Nano-RES concentration in both of the binary and ternary cases at Δλ = 60 nm. The outcomes confirmed the binding of Hb and Hb-HTF complex in the presence of Nano-RES and approved the placement of Hb in a single or complex state with HTF around the Trp residues. According to the insets of Fig. 8A and B, the enhancement of Nano-RES throughout the binary and ternary systems at Δλ = 15 nm caused a considerable decline in the fluorescence intensity, which suggests the binding of Nano-RES with Hb and Hb-HTF while being situated in the vicinity of Tyr residues. As a noteworthy fact, the unchanged state of Trp and Tyr residues micro environment was confirmed by the lack of observing any changes in the red or blue shifts of both cases. Fig. 8C and 8D represent the curves of F/F0 versus Nano-RES concentrations regarding the Hb and Hb-HTF complex at Δλ = 15 nm and Δλ = 60 nm, respectively. The obtained results demonstrated a higher slop for Hb-Nano-RES system at Δλ = 60 as a sign of the inducement of greater changes in the vicinity of Trp residues microenvironment than Tyr residue. Similar outcomes were recorded for the Hb-HTF complex in the presence of Nano-RES as the ternary system.

Resonance light scattering (RLS) is referred to the inducement of an elastic dispersion upon the closeness of an incident beam in energy to the absorption band. This interesting method can be used to determine molecular assemblies and chromophores aggregates [51]. The RLS spectra were provided through the simultaneous scanning of excitation and emission wavelengths between the range of 220 to 600 nm at Δλ = 0 nm, which can be accounted through the following equation [52]:
(7)
I =
32
π
3
V
2
n
2
N
3
λ
0
4
[
δ n
2
+
δ k
2
]
In which n stands for the refractive index of medium, N presents the molarity of solution, λ is the incident and scattered light wavelength, V expresses the molecular volume, and δn and δk are the fluctuations of the real and imaginary components of particles refractive index, respectively.

See Fig. 9 A and 9B demonstrate the RLS spectra of Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES complexes, respectively. The observance of an evident increase in the RLS signal of Hb and Hb-HTF in the appearance of Nano-RES concentrations suggests the formation of a new complex with a greater size by the interaction of ligand with both proteins in the binary and ternary systems. Accordingly, Fig. 9C depicts the sigmoidal curve of ΔIRLS against Nano-RES concentration, in which ΔIRLS = IRLS – I0 RLS as IRLS and I0 RLS refer to the RLS intensities in the presence and absence of the ligand. It is crucial to determine the CCIAC (critical induced aggregation concentrations) parameter to gain a better understanding on the behavior of colloidal systems. In conformity to Fig. 9C, since the evaluation of CCIAC canpresent the formation of particle clusters through different interparticle forces, the obtained data from ΔIRLS curve displayed an increase in the CCIAC values of the ternary system. This observation expresses the potential of HTF to reduce the binding affinity of Nano-RES into the formation of aggregations with Hb. Moreover, the exhibition of two continuous sigmoidal plotin the ternary complex signifies the existence of two sets of binding sites for Nano-RES in Hb-HTF complex system. Therefore, it is indicated that the appearance of HTF changed the interaction behavior of Nano-RES.

Circular dichroism (CD) is a spectroscopic technique for gathering data about the asymmetric features of proteins backbone and is extensively exerted for the assessment of proteins conformation and stability in different conditions such as temperature, ionic strength, and presence of small molecules [53]. The properties of far-UV CD spectra can determine the structural changes of proteins secondary structure including α-helix, β −sheet, and a random coil. The α-helix involves two negative bands at 222 and 208 nm, and a positive band at ∼ 190 nm, while the spectrum of β-sheet is generally recorded as a negative band in the range of 210–220 nm and a positive band in the range of 195–200 nm [54]. In addition, random coil exhibits a negative band at around 200 nm [55]. As it can be perceived from Fig. 10 , the characteristics of α-helix represented two negative bands at 208 and 222 nm throughout the UV region. The isodichroic point in Fig. 10 (A, B) revealed the equilibrium between Hb and Nano-RES as binary system and Hb-HTF with Nano-RES as ternary system that showed the physical forces between proteins and Nano-RES as binary and ternary systems. Table 2 demonstrates the fraction of Hb and Hb-HTF secondary structure in the appearance of Nano-RES at varying concentrations (0.0025, 0.005, 0.0075, and 0.01 mM) as the binary and ternary systems. In conformity to Table 2, the binding of Nano-RES to Hb and Hb-HTF complex resulted in decreasing the negative ellipticity of all the wavelengths, which indicates the enhancing effect of Nano-RES on the secondary contents of proteins. This observation also confirmed the occurrence of unfolding process through the binding of ligand to both proteins in the binary and ternary systems. Table 2 reveals the lower content of the secondary structure of Hb-HTF complex than Hb upon interaction with Nano-RES and consequently suggested the greater stability of this complex, since HTF can lead to the creation of more bonds content in Hb at the appearance of Nano-RES. In conclusion, the interaction of Hb and HTF with Nano-RES was configured as an equilibrium process, meaning the interactions are reversible and the participated bonds in the reaction are not covalent. Therefore, the proteins alternations are in a positive direction towards the structural and functional enhancement of proteins.

Melting temperature (Tm) is defined as the temperature in which the folded and unfolded states of the protein are equally at equilibrium. Therefore, it is a crucial measurement to determine the thermal resistance of proteins [56]. Hence, this assessment was excreted to understand the effect of Nano-RES on the thermal stability of Hb and HTF as the binary and ternary systems. Fig. 11 demonstrates the plot of relative absorbance at 280 nm against temperature. According to Fig. 11A, the Tm values of Hb in the absence and presence of Nano-RES were 53 °C and 50 °C, respectively, which indicates the reducing impact of Nano-RES on thermal stability. Meanwhile, the measurements of Hb-HTF and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES complex as the ternary system were obtained to be 56.6 °C and 61.5 °C, suggesting the enhancing effect of Nano-RES on the thermal stability of proteins. The presence of interaction behavior between Hb and HTF due to short-range and long-range interactions causes the stability of Hb, and the interaction of Nano-RES due to the hiding of binding sites will stabilize the ternary system compared to the binary system. Therefore, the presence of HTF can empower the thermal stability of a complex and this alternation can be contemplated as a positive effect on increasing the functional rate of proteins.

The conductometry measurements of Hb and Hb-HTF complexes were completed in the presence of Nano-RES. This technique is capable of determining the electrical current conduction. According to the Ohm's law E = IR, in which I present the electric current, R refers to the resistance, and E represents the potential difference, conductivity can be defined as the following [57]:
(8)
G
=
1 / R

Hence, the enhancement of solutions conductivity can intensify the electric current conduction. The two main applications of conductometry include the configuration of total conductivity in a solution and the endpoints of titrations, which involve ions determination [58]. Fig. 12 depicts the conductance plot of Hb and Hb-HTF in the presence of different Nano-RES concentrations. Since, the electrical conductivity of a solution is based on its number of ions, the conductivity enhancement of Hb and Hb-HTF solution may be correlated with the transferring of ionizable groups such as the –OH of Tyr residues. Therefore, the creation of a conformational change by ionizable group’s transmission signifies the occurrence of interactions between a ligand and proteins. As it is implied by Fig. 12, the molar conductivity of Hb and Hb-HTF complex solution faced a moderate increase along with the addition of Nano-RES concentrations as a sign of complex formation in both cases of binary and ternary systems. The molar conductivity differences of binary and ternary systems indicate the different behavior of Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES complexes, as well as the conformational changes of ligand–protein interactions. Moreover, two sets of binding sites were observed throughout the ternary system, which confirmed the data of RLS measurements.

The oxy-hemoglobin dissociation curve (commonly known as a sigmoid plot) shows the ratio of saturated hemoglobin on the vertical axis against the dominant tension of oxygen on the horizontal axis to provide information about blood carries and oxygen releases [59]. Fig. 13 A displays the ODC curve of Hb in the absence and presence of Nano-RES in two different concentrations (0.005 and 0.01 mM). Clearly, ODC curve faced a transfer in the presence of Nano-RES as the affirming sign of Hb binding to Nano-RES, which also occurred in the case of ternary system. In conformity to the obtained data, the interaction caused a reduction in the affinity of hemoglobin for oxygen and also facilitated the conditions for amore facile oxygen release throughout the tissues at a certain pressure, meaning that the presence of Nano-RES increased the rate of cell oxygenation process.

Controlled release drug delivery systems are designed to ensure the release of specified amounts of entrapped drug over an appointed timeline. Hence, this study exerted simulated gastrointestinal sequential digestion to investigate the in vitro releasing behavior of Resveratrol-loaded mesoporous silica nanoparticles (RES-MSNs) [60], Resveratrol-loaded solid lipid nanoparticles (RES-SLNs) [61], Nano-RES prepared by O/W binding to Hb, and Nano-RES prepared by O/W binding to Hb-HTF. According to Fig. 14 , the first 2 h of gastric digestion revealed the cumulative percentages of around 20 % for Nano-RES release in all the cases, while after 6 h of digestion (2 h in SGF at pH = 1.2 and 4 h in SIF at pH = 7.5), the released percentages were measured to be around 62 %, 50 %, 35 %, and 30 %, respectively. In comparison to RES-MSNs and RES-SLNs, the results demonstrated a significantly slower release of Nano-RES prepared by O/W binding to Hb and Nano-RES prepared by O/W binding to Hb-HTF. It can be concluded that the preparation of Nano-RES through O/W method and binding to Hb and Hb-HTF proved to be more suitable than RES-MSNs and RES-SLNs for gaining control over the drug releases, which is quite important in drug delivery systems.

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer is a non-radiative mechanism for transferring energy from a donor fluorophore in an electronically excited state to an acceptor fluorophore in an electronically grounded state by dipole–dipole coupling. The efficiency of FRET is dependent on therdistance of the donor and acceptor that can be determined through the following equation [62]:
(9)
E F R E T = R 60 / R 60 + r 6
Where R0 is the Förster radius and represents the distance between donor and acceptor fluorophore for 50 % FRET efficiency, which correlates to the molecular orientation of the fluorophores. The overlap between the donor emission and acceptor absorption spectrum can be expressed by the equation below [63]:
(10)
R 60 =
8.8
×
10 - 25
K 2
N - 4 Ф ⌡ ]
Herein, K2 refers to the spatial orientation factor of dipole, N represents the refractive index of medium, Ф expresses the fluorescence quantum yield of donor, and J is the overlap integral of fluorescence emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor, which is determined as the following [64]:
(11)
J =
∑
F ( λ )
ε
( λ )
λ 4 d λ
/
∑
F ( λ )
d ( λ )

9848853159760 Table 3 displays all the values of E, J, R0, and r for the cases of Hb and Hb-HTF in the appearance of Nano-RES throughout the binary and ternary systems. According to this table, the distances of donor and acceptor were calculated to be 1.68 and 1.77 nm for Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES complexes, respectively, revealing the lower r distances than 7 nm. These data confirmed the interaction of Nano-RES with both proteins as the binary and ternary systems and signified the participation of static quenching mechanism. The differences of r values in the case of binary and ternary systems implied the greater distance of the donor and acceptor in a ternary system, suggesting the impact of conformational changes on the inducement of different behavior interactions. Fig. 15 displays the overlap of Nano-RES absorption spectrum on the fluorescence emission spectrum of Hb and Hb-HTF complexes, respectively, which reveals the probable occurrence of energy transfer between the donor and acceptor in both cases.

Due to the dependence of many biological and chemical features on molecular interactions, isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) turned into a distinctive method as an analytical tool for gathering quantitative thermodynamic data [65]. Since is rely on As a result of its ability to determine the heat of molecular reactions, ITC can acquire the macromolecular complex formation energy that occurthrough the interactions of drugs with target biomolecules. The value of binding isotherm can be derived through binding enthalpy (ΔH0), the equilibrium binding affinity (Ka = 1/Kd), and the number of binding sites N; therefore, the following formula can calculate the Gibbs free energy [66]:
(12)
Δ G 0
=
R T l n K d

According to the Ka and ΔH0 values in Table 4 , the reaction turned into a more entropy-driven state due to the favorable enthalpy and entropy values, signifying the dominance of electrostatic interactions. Fig. 16 A and B depicts the ITC evaluations of the Hb-Nano-RES and (Hb-HTF) Nano-RES bindings, respectively. Evidently, the rate of heating was assessed to maintain a constant cell temperature during the titration process. A calorimetric titration implicates a direct correlation between the heating rate and the ligand binding fraction. Therefore, the integration of heating rate led to the induction of interaction process throughout the titration step. A mathematical model was exerted to analyze the continuous heat titration data. In conformity to Fig. 16, the injection of initial Nano-RES titration to reference cells (including proteins) pointed out a reduction in the released heat of the samples through the ITC data. Each injection faced the occurrence of interactions between the protein and the ligand, which ultimately led to the creation of endothermic or exothermic signals. The exhibited downward peaks in Fig. 16 indicated the exothermic state of the interaction between the ligand and the protein. Table 4 displays the Ka values of both Hb and Hb-HTF complexes in the presence of Nano-RES, which are very close to the mentioned Ksv values in Table 1 and represent the confirmation of these results with fluorescence measurements.

The ligand contained a molecular weight of less than 500 g/mol as it is included in the data of Table 5 , which can provide the facile permeation ofcell membranes since lower values result in the production of smaller molecules. Additionally, Log p of the ligand was also observed to be less than 5 in association with hydrophobicity. This parameter denotes the dissolving capacity of a chemical compound in oils, fats, and non-polar solvents. HBD and HBA ligand values were less than 5 and 10, respectively, indicating the biological effect of medications on the physicochemical properties of compounds such as melting points, boiling points, water solubility and acidity; however, the reported number of rotatable bonds were less than 10 [41].

Toxicology is a critical issue in the creation of new medications. For instance, the safety concerns of several pharmaceutical products resulted in their removal from the market and wasted years of work, money, and effort that was put on their drug development programs [67–69]. Therefore, ProTox-II online server was used to check the probability of hepatotoxicity, carcinogenicity, immunotoxicity, mutagenicity, cytotoxicity, and inhibition of Tox21 nuclear receptor signaling pathways, as well as to calculate the probable inhibition of Tox21 stress response pathways in order to assess the potential toxicity of RES. According to Table 6 , it was unexpended to observe any hepatotoxicity from the ligand, which also lacked any carcinogenic, immunotoxic, mutagenic, or cytotoxic potential. RES was unable to inhibit Tox21 nuclear receptor signalling pathways and Tox21 stress response pathways next to the absence of any deleterious effects (except AR, ER, ER-LBD and MMP pathways).

Molecular docking experiment was carried out to study the exact binding location of RES on Hb. Fig. 17 demonstrates the lowest free energy of Hb-RES system as the best ranked result. The docking outcomes confirmed the crucial role of hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions throughout the complexation process of Hb-RES system. Furthermore, RES was observed to be surrounded by Lys40, Thr41, Phe43, Pro44, Phe46, Asp47, Lue48, Ser49, His97, Cys93 and His146. The RES ligand can form a hydrogen bond with Cys93, and one pi-H bond with the Leu48 residues of Hb, with the bond length of 2.93 Å and 3.86 Å and the energy values of −0.7 and −0.5 kcal/mol, respectively. The computational studies of Hb-RES system resulted in ΔG0 = -5.35 kJ/mol. The best docking interaction of RES and Hb-HTF complex expressed the most befitting energy score among the other cases and was observed to be −6.31 kJ/mol. Fig. 18 exhibits the best binding site that involved a negative interaction energy, which also pointed out the embedding of RES in the HTF protein. According to the protein structure, the ligand created hydrophobic and hydrogen bonds in the C lobe of the protein, indicating the formation of hydrogen bonds with Ser189 and Leu294 residues by RES with the energy values of of −0.9 and −2.4 kcal/mol, respectively. Table 7 reports the docking results of binary and ternary systems [70,71].

The stability of the complexes was evaluated by calculating the root mean square deviations (RMSD) of the protein C-alpha from the reference structure during the simulation time. The average distance between the atoms of the protein was measured through the RMSD technique in order to examine the extend of structural changes in the protein [72,73]. Molecular dynamics simulation of Hb-RES exhibited a strong stability during the 100 ns time, while the RES was observed in a stable position at its binding site. The RMSDs of binary system was indicative of an overall stability throughout the100 ns of simulation (Fig. 19 A). As depicted in Fig. 18B, the achievement of an average RMSD (0.553) referred to the unsatisfying binding stability of RES in Hb-HTF. The global dynamic behavior of the protein was significantly altered upon its binding to RES. The radius of gyration is another parameter for describing the equilibrium conformation of proteins by measuring the structural compactness. The stability of a proteinin its fold sustains the relative constant state of Rg, whereas its unfolding leads to the gradual fluctuation of Rg [74,75]. In coordination to Fig. 20 A, the Rg values of Hb-RES complex reached a plateau at around 95 ns, which is another proof of its equilibrium state. The lower Rg values of Hb in its complex form are attributed to the induced conformational changes by RES that consequently result in a somewhat more compact structure. The Rg value of Hb-HTF in a ternary system reached a plateau after 20 ns until the end of simulation, while the complex graph equilibrated subsequent to 50 ns and further decreased after 90 ns (Fig. 20B).

The cytotoxic activity of Nano-RES was evaluated through an MTT procedure, which resulted in observing its conformation to the applied volume and its time-dependent cytotoxicity in A549 and BEAS-2B cells (alveolar and bronchial epithelial cell lines, respectively)in the appearance of Nano-RES at different concentrations (0–0.01 mM) for 48 h. Fig. 21 depicts the occurrence ofareduction in the viability of both A549 and BEAS-2B cells by the exerted Nano-RES. In Fig. 21, we present the results demonstrating a dose dependent reduction in cell viability. Significantly, our results demonstrate that the treatment had a notable effect on cell viability, as evidenced by a dose dependent reduction in the viability of the treated culture cells. In MTT assay, the cytotoxicity activity in A549 and BEAS-2B cells was significally observed at 48 h after treatment of Nano-RES. The results indicated that at the IC50 concentrations of 0.00484 mM and 0.006 mM of Nano-RES, the proliferation of A549 and BEAS-2B cells were significantly eliminated. Clearly, despite the inconsiderable changes of cell viability upon the usage of 0–0.002 mM of Nano-RES, there was a greater reduction after the application of 0.01 mM that was measured to be 20 % and 30 % for A549 and BEAS-2B cells, respectively.

Fig. 22 A depicts the enhancing effect of varying concentrations of Nano-RES (0–0.01 mM) on the apoptosis of A549 cells throughout 48 h. Accordingly, the percentage of apoptotic cells were increased by enlarging the applied dosage of Nano-RES, which was observed to be less than 10 % at 0 mM and about 70 % at 0.01 mM. Due to the role of Bax and Bcl-2 as the main proteins in the intrinsic pathway of apoptosis, we further assessed the effects of Nano-RES on cell cytotoxicity and exerted real time PCR analyses to evaluate their folding as tumor suppressor genes at mRNA and protein levels. Fig. 22B and 22C contain the results of qRT-PCR technique to present the transcriptional expressions of Bcl-2 and Bax, respectively. The noteworthy point of Fig. 22B is the recorded increase in the level expression of Bax mRNA (known as up regulation) induced by the different concentrations of Nano-RES, as it reached its highest value at 0.01 mM. Meanwhile, the mRNA expression of Bcl-2 was reduced (known as down regulation) throughout the treatment of A549 cells with Nano-RES (Fig. 22C). Considering the occurrence of similar procedures in the folding of Bax and Bcl-2 proteins (Fig. 22D and 22E), the down regulation of PI3K-AKT signalling pathway was configured as the mechanism of apoptosis in accordance to these data.

This research investigated the interaction of Hb and Hb-HTF complex in the presence of Nano-RES as the forms of binary and ternary systems through the means of multi spectroscopic techniques and the conduction of cellular experiments. The fluorescence spectroscopy outcomes displayed a reduction in fluorescence quenching and therefore affirmed the complex formation of the ligand with both proteins. Also, the linear Stern-Volmer plot indicated the participation of a single type of quenching in the interaction and confirmed the occurrence of an static quenching. The outcomes of thermodynamic parameters indicate the involvement of electrostatic forces throughout the interactions. In addition, the RLS results supported the production of larger complexes in both binary and ternary systems. The data of circular dichroism technique determined the higher secondary structural content of ternary system than the binary system and therefore suggested the greater stability of Hb-HTF complex. The Tm values of ternary system in the presence of Nano-RES seemed to have faced a more fundamental structural transition than the binary system. This phenomenon indicates the superior positive effect of Nano-RES on the thermal stability of ternary system. Moreover, the molar conductivity was gradually increased upon the addition of Nano-RES and therefore supported the outcomes of RLS measurements. The observance of downward peaks in the ITC measurements indicated the occurrence of an exothermic interaction between the ligand and proteins. The recorded right shift in the ODC plots of both binary and ternary systems confirmed the successful binding of ligand and protein and more oxygen supply to the tissues. In comparison to the RES-MSNs and RES-SLNs, the data of in vitro release behaviour exhibited the significantly slower releasing rate of prepared Nano-RES by o/w binding to Hb and Hb-HTF. In conclusion, Hb and HTF can stand as appropriate carrier proteins for Nano-RES in protein–ligand interactions due to the suitable binding behavior of Nano-RES to proteins regulation.

Shiva Masoudnia: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Alireza Motavalizadehkakhki: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jamshid Mehrzad: Formal analysis. Malihesadat Hoseini: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Jamshidkhan Chamani: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization.
